ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Sayur Asam; A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Sayur Asam is approximately 350 g, comprising tamarind broth and a generous mix of vegetables including corn, long beans, and chayote.

nutrient_source.title: Sayur Asam (Vegetable Tamarind Soup) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for individual vegetables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single FAO/INFOODS or HPB entry exists for the exact Singaporean restaurant version of Sayur Asam. Values are estimated by weighted composition of the primary ingredients (tamarind broth ~70%, mixed vegetables ~30%) using FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for corn, long beans, chayote, peanuts, and tamarind. The estimate reflects a typical restaurant-style serving with moderate sodium from added salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable soup with tamarind
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sayur Asam. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous food category: mixed vegetable soup with legumes and tamarind broth. Tamarind contributes organic acids which may lower glycemic response. Long beans and chayote are low-GI vegetables. Analogous reference: vegetable soups with legumes, GI ~30–40, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 4.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 5.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and tamarind broth
hypertension.guidance: Sayur Asam contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (735 mg per standard 350 g bowl), which represents about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high vegetable fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, Sayur Asam is a suitable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the dietary fibre from long beans, chayote, and corn supports slower glucose absorption and improved postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based ingredients, and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sayur Asam is low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol from animal sources, making it a heart-friendly choice; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk through sodium restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sayur Asam is a traditional Indonesian and Singaporean vegetable soup characterised by a tangy, sour tamarind-based broth. The dish typically contains a variety of vegetables including corn on the cob, long beans (kacang panjang), chayote (labu siam), melinjo leaves, tomato, and peanuts, simmered in a broth seasoned with shallots, galangal, bay leaves, and tamarind. The result is a light, refreshing, mildly sour soup with a clean vegetable flavour and a slight nuttiness from peanuts. Sayur Asam is a staple of Betawi (Jakarta) cuisine and is widely consumed across Indonesia and in Indonesian-influenced food stalls in Singapore. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides dietary fibre, potassium, and modest amounts of vitamins A and C from the vegetables. The low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) makes it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate due to added salt in restaurant preparation, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically served as a side soup alongside rice and protein dishes such as fried chicken or tempeh in a Nasi Padang or Nasi Campur setting.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Sayur Asam was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a single composite dish entry. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for the individual components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for mixed vegetable soups with legumes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 70% tamarind broth and 30% mixed vegetables (corn, long beans, chayote, peanuts, melinjo, tomato). Sodium is estimated at 210 mg/100 g reflecting typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions may be lower. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS plant food composition data. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in this plant-based soup; confirmed zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used; confirmed zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from peanut and vegetable oil minor contributions. GI set to 35 (low) as an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable soup with legumes per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sayur Asam exists in the literature. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (35 × 4.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 5.1. The dish is served at Dapur Penyet - Funan (field survey data) at SGD 8.00. FoodOn match FOODON_03311737 (vegetable soup) is a closest_parent match; no species-specific FoodOn entry exists for Sayur Asam. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Soups and broths) is a closest_parent match.
